sentences.text: Yes.
When we were told that we have to leave the barracks, that we're not going to go to work, we saw smoke coming out from a distance from Gross-Rosen.
And we also heard rumors that the Russian army is approaching that part of Germany, and that's why they are taking us out of Graben.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the death march, we weren't given any food.
For-- we were not sure where they were driving us.
There were 250 girls, mainly from Sosnowiec and [POLISH], from the vicinity.
There were-- I knew-- I know-- we knew there were 250 because of the number of girls assigned to each room and the number of rooms we had in the camp.
We weren't given any food for several days.
And--
We were wearing just the clothes we wore when we were going to go to the [NON-ENGLISH] factory.
But they did tell us to take if we have some clothes.
I remember, some warm clothes.
I think I recall that.
It was in January.
Or we, ourselves, felt that we should.
They didn't tell us.
We, ourselves, felt that we wanted to be warm.
It was a cold day, and we took warm clothes.
If we had coats, we took coats along.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not sure how long the death march lasted.
But having given no food, we were weakened.
And we were driven to go faster and faster.
And when one of us-- our three sisters-- wasn't feeling well, we would take her in the middle, and the two on the outside would kind of drag her along.
And somehow, we made it to open cattle wagons.
They loaded us onto open cattle wagons.
Before they did, they gave us some food.
There was a bowl of soup given to each one of us.
We didn't sleep.
We were just marching day and night.
We saw them shooting those of us who couldn't go on walking.
The German guards would shoot on the spot.
And actually, being afraid of being shot, we just kept on going.
They loaded us.
They gave us some food and loaded us on open cattle wagons.
And the snow was falling.
And next to us, there was a man from Gross-Rosen.
And they were telling us that they were sleeping on top of dead corpses because they've been on those wagons for some time.
And we-- we were kept separately.
But the adjoining wagon, we could just make out some words.
And they also were very desperate.
And it was very crowded in the cattle wagons.
And I'm not sure how long we were there.
But it was-- the wagons would stop and go on again dragging on.
And there was a lot of suffering.
None of us died in the wagons.
But on the march, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were 250 left Graben.
And when we were unloaded from the wagons, there were 150 left of us.
So they shot about 100 girls.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Do you want me to tell about the Bergen-Belsen?
Please.
They told us to get out of the wagons.
And we were taken into a camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] called Bergen-Belsen.
That's where Anne Frank died.
And I was kind of the same age that as Anne Frank was at the time.
We were taken into showers.
And we had to leave our clothes that we were wearing, and they gave us-- gave us different clothes.
And they drove us into one room.
And we were just stacked in sitting position, 150 girls.
We couldn't lay down.
We could barely stand up.
We just had to be remain sitting.
We were given some food.
And the following day, there was an appell, to go out to the yard in Bergen-Belsen.
And after the appell, a girl by the name of [NON-ENGLISH] came over to my sister, and she knew her from Sosnowiec.
And she says, Esther, you're here.
So my sister said, yes, and my sisters.
This girl was in Auschwitz, and she was transferred-- she survived Auschwitz-- to Bergen-Belsen.
And she became a [NON-ENGLISH].
It was kind of a group leader.
And she had a lot of influence in Bergen-Belsen.
And she told Esther, I'm going to help you with whatever I can.
And she assigned my sister [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to work.
And those who worked sorting out clothes-- those who worked were able to move into a room where there were bunk beds.
That if you could sleep-- I mean, that was a far relief from being stapled in like staples in a sitting position in one room with 150 girls.
She took us out of that room.
She assigned my sisters to sort out clothing, and she assigned me to work in the kitchen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And our lot has improved greatly by being able to get a night's sleep and going to work.
We were given food but not adequate food.
And we would exchange.
My sisters took some clothes out and brought it to the room and exchanged it with other people at the camp for food, for bread, or potatoes.
So we had additional food in addition to the food we were given in Bergen-Belsen.
I would take food and put it in the-- my coat, the bottom of my-- the lining.
I would put a couple of potatoes in.
But I was actually risking my life, because sometimes they were searching us leaving the kitchen.
I did this a couple of times, and then I gave up.
And the people who stole paid with their life.
And I will never forget one instance.
As I was leaving the kitchen to go home to the barracks, there was a large pile of raw potatoes outside in the yard.
It was a very large area.
And a guard was kind of watching the girls leave the kitchen.
And at that time, I saw a man bending down.
He was wearing striped clothes.
Apparently, he was in Auschwitz.
He was wearing striped clothes and looked emaciated.
And he bent down, wanting to pick up a potato.
When the German guard noticed that, he shot him on the spot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] kind of-- I couldn't believe that I saw this with my own eyes.
And now thinking back, he could have just told him, don't take it.
You're not allowed to take it.
But he just shot him.
And the expression on his face was expression of a guy who did something great.
He was-- seemed to have a smirk and half a smile on his face.
Yes.
I was very, very close.
And later on, I just thought, how I could a man be so brutal?
Was he not born out of a-- like we all were born?
Does he have no sisters or brothers?
Did he have no mother?
What kind of a tyrant would do that?
And to this day, I still wonder how they could carry out the atrocities which they did.
I've seen people shot-- the girls who couldn't.
They kind of left them behind, and we they-- we heard the shots, but we did not actually see it.
But this I was witnessed from a very short distance.
From a short-- yes.
That I really saw with my eyes.
We discussed everything.
And we-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we-- it was decided I shouldn't bring anymore.
It's hard to talk about these things, you know.
To-- even so, I'm trying very hard not to break.
We were actually hoping, talking about survival, and being united with our parents and the rest of the family.
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] conditions were so bad in Bergen-Belsen.
That typhus was raging-- dysentery, typhus.
And I was the first one of the three of us to succumb to typhus.
And naturally, I stopped going to work.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was-- my sister Mary may have been the first one and then I.
But I had a very severe case.
And when I-- the toilets were just spilling over.
And one night, when I went out-- because I was just searching for a place [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the backyard.
And the next thing I knew, that I was on top of corpses.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just touched around.
It was like slippery.
And I don't know how I got there.
But realizing that I was among dead people, I gathered all my strength, and I got myself out of that position, and crawled-- got back to the room where my sisters were.
And later on, my sister, Esther, who was like a mother to me, saw that I was so sick that she took me to a hospital.
A kind of [NON-ENGLISH], that's called.
Because she thought that I would die anyway.
And she thought perhaps I could get some help where they keep very sick people.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
My sister kept visiting me in the evenings.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I couldn't eat anything.
And then she would tell me one day, what do you really feel you would want to eat?
And I said to her, [POLISH].
That means potato pancakes, because that was my favorite food, and it was also my-- my sister's favorite food.
Later on, she said she found a piece of metal.
She and my sister, Mary, made holes in the metal.
And somehow, they had some potatoes, and they grated the potatoes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she brought the [POLISH].
Oh, she's-- I'm not quite sure now.
I'm quite confused, because later on, she told me that some Ukrainian women attacked her and took away the food that she was going to give me.
I'm not sure it was the following night.
I now don't recall whether she brought me the [POLISH] or not.
Maybe she did once.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But there was chaos in camp, and the strong ones attacked the weak ones.
And the Ukrainian women were very strong and husky, and they attacked her.
But she came one night-- now, I recall-- to the hospital.
I had a bed there.
And she didn't bring any food.
She didn't tell me why.
But later on, after the war, she recalled and she told me that story.
They were mostly healthy.
Healthy ones the Germans chose.
Healthy men and women.
We were not.
We were broken people.
We were just nearing the end, so to speak.
Yes, beds, barracks.
And we were the lucky ones to have beds, because the remaining girls stayed in that room, and most of them died.
A very small percentage of the 150 girls survived.
Somehow, I apparently improved.
My health improved.
And then my sister, Esther, succumbed to the sickness, high fever, and diarrhea.
And I was feeling better.
And my sister, Mary, was still in between.
And then I heard rumors the war ended.
The English are here.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to tell my sister, who was lying sick.
I said, Esther, we are liberated.
The English are here.
It didn't make an impression on her.
She was kind of far gone, it seems.
But later-- later on, she regained consciousness.
Her health improved.
And we were told that our brother is in the same camp, our brother, Simon.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because there were lists of surviving people looking for one another.
Even in Bergen-Belsen, in the camp, there were lists of names.
And then one girl came to tell my sister and told her.
And that woke her up-- that Simon is alive.
And she regained consciousness.
And then we were told that they're waiting for us behind barbed wires.
They were kind of freed, and they had freedom of movement.
But we did not realize that we were free, and we were too sick--
To leave.
To leave.
We were told at that point that they poisoned the water.
Had the English not arrived on that day-- I think it was April, the latter part of April or middle part-- that we would all have died.
The Germans poisoned the water.
And there were no Germans to be seen, only scattered-- scattered we saw some.
And we wanted to be with our brother.
We were told that he is in the same camp, but he's not allowed to enter our camp because of the typhoid is raging.
So he was-- they separated us.
But we could see him if we walked to the barbed wire.
And the three of us just walked from a distance and we saw.
And as we were approaching my brother, I couldn't make it.
I was so weak and so emaciated that I had to sit down even though I wanted to see him very badly.
But I couldn't make it in one trip.
I'm not sure if it was 50 yards before the place where he was waiting for us.
And then my brother came.
Or he told us he took us out of Bergen-Belsen.
I think there was a town called Hanover close by.
And he rented or took a room, or some of his friends, and we left the camp.
I saw the English probably the following day of their arrival.
And they were throwing chocolates and goodies to us.
They didn't want to go too close to us, so they stayed in their Jeeps.
And I said to my sister, I hear that they're giving food there and chocolate.
I said, I'm going out to get something.
But I was so weak that I couldn't get to the front, and the strong ones got the goodies.
And when I came back to the room, it was two kohlrabies.
Because they were easy to get, easy-- easy access to the kohlrabi.
And thinking that even kohlrabi is good, because-- and we were given kohlrabi in camp.
And this is kind of became a joke.
Sally is coming with the kohlrabi.
I'm not quite sure, but our brother took us out of the-- of the camp.
And not quite sure what happened to the Germans.
We were most likely from January, February, to April.
A couple of months.
To the inmates?
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I personally did not experience any beating.
But they were cruel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That-- it was death for anything you did that you're not supposed to do.
There was a crematorium there.
Because I found out later that the stack of corpses that I was on was going to be cremated.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I somehow-- maybe they picked me up and they thought that I was dead.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how I got there, but it was a cruel place.
Then lived in a room someplace with my brother's friends.
And the boys would go out and get food from German farms.
They would just go in and say, give me this, and give me that.
And the Germans raised their hand.
And when they asked where they came from, they told them, we came from Bergen-Belsen, the camp.
Oh, we didn't know anything about it.
We don't know what happened.
We don't know.
But take what you want to take, because they were afraid of being harmed by the former inmates.
So then stayed in Hanover.
And we found out that two of my other brothers survived in Buchenwald.
Which two brothers?
That was Itzak and Nathan.
So we boarded some trains and we went to Buchenwald, which was the American part of Germany.
And we stayed there in a DP camp for a few weeks or a couple of months.
And we were given good food, and we started to recuperate.
And we had our own kitchen like for maybe 20 people, and we could make our own breakfast.
So life changed for us completely, naturally.
We were free people.
I personally did not go back to Sosnowiec, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't.
When we found out that the parents are not alive, I just couldn't do it to go back to the home where we used to live.
I don't-- didn't think I was strong enough.
But one of our brothers did-- Itzak did.
And he saw some of the neighbors.
And he came back broken.
And we heard that the Poles then attacked the survivors.
And they said, you're still alive?
You survived?
But I personally talked to a lady who was-- who went back to Poland.
She was pregnant.
It was maybe eight months or a year after the war ended.
And they attacked her and injured her so that she lost the baby.
So at that stage, we decided not to go back to Poland.
Was your brother attacked?
My brother wasn't attacked.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the decision was made not to go back and not ever to go back.
Because what to go back for if there are no survivors?
And we decided never to live in Poland again.
And we also decided we wanted to leave Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- none of us had any profession or trade.
So at the DP camp, we started learning trades through the Ort, O-R-T schools.
And back in Buchenwald, we heard that the Russians were going to take over that part of Germany.
And we decided to leave Buchenwald with our brothers.
There were five of us then out of 13.
My-- 10 siblings and my parents were 13 people.
We-- five of us-- three brothers-- three sisters.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Actually, there were three brothers survived.
One we lost later.
Six survived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, we decided to leave Buchenwald.
And we settled-- we were given trains by, I think, the Joint Distribution Committee.
And they helped us with transportation to the DP camp Landsberg am Lech in Bavaria.
We were there till about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '45, '49 or so.
My brother was given a position as a [NON-ENGLISH], brother Simon.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was in Germany, [GERMAN] in Bavaria.
And he managed a textile shop, because he was familiar with textiles.
Yes, please.
